data.title: Why Lumen's Impressive Rally May Be Running Out Of Steam
data.description:
Lumen Technologies Is An Interesting Turnaround Story In The Works

Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN) stock has materially underperformed the S&P500 for more than a decade:

This is primarily due to secular decline in the legacy telecom (landline) business and a large acquisition of Level 3 Communications that arguably failed to realize anticipated synergies and riddled the company with high debt.

But now, the company is trying to stage a turnaround by pivoting away from being a legacy wireline telecom carrier into a data center network infrastructure partner for hyperscalers via private connectivity fabric (PCF) deals. These PCF deals involve providing hyperscalers with exclusive custom network infrastructure for their data center needs over a long period of time (20 years). Strategically, the company is leaning toward targeting this enterprise market whilst it scales out of its consumer fiber business to free up cash and ease its debt burden.

This is what the business mix looks like:

The big story is in the Business Grow segment; this is where the company is pivoting toward providing more emerging enterprise networking technologies such as dark fiber and Edge Cloud. Business Nurture relates to more mature enterprise solutions such as Ethernet and VPN. Business Harvest refers to the legacy enterprise business lines in older voice communication technologies that are becoming outdated vs the modern voice over IP type technologies. Business other includes telecom equipment sales and professional services. Lumen's consumer business spans its fiber (most of which is getting divested) and other broadband business, as well as legacy landline voice services.

Thesis

I have a balanced view on LUMN stock right now:

  1. The PCF deal flow momentum is promising.
  2. But Lumen's revenues are expected to remain under pressure.
  3. Leverage related metrics are a key risk monitorable.
  4. Valuations are frothy.
  5. LUMN is posting a genuine bullish reversal, but momentum is slowing down.

The PCF Deal Flow Momentum Is Promising

Lumen has locked in over $10 billion worth of PCF deals with major hyperscalers such as Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) and Amazon (AMZN). It is also gaining strong traction in its enterprise network as a service (NaaS) business:

But the real headline for this earnings call is the progress we're making to pivot this company back to growth -- revenue growth. We signed an additional $1-plus billion in private connectivity fabric deals since our last update, bringing the total PCF deal value to over $10 billion. We continue to scale the adoption of NaaS, reaching more than 1,500 enterprise customers since the launch of this platform. We launched our latest NaaS innovation, Internet on-demand or IOD off-net, giving us nearly 100x greater market reach to accelerate digital service sales and revenue growth. We're rapidly building a connected ecosystem with dozens of early adopter tech partners who see how Lumen's digital platform can accelerate their time to value with joint customers.

- CEO Kathleen Johnson in the Q3 FY25 earnings call.

Currently, Lumen has the first-mover advantage as other large telecom companies such as AT&T (T), T-Mobile (TMUS) and Verizon (VZ) are yet to complete their fiber network buildouts to capture the enterprise PCF opportunity. I believe the next 1 year in particular will be critical for Lumen; the more exclusive, long-term contracts it can sign with hyperscalers in this time, the more resistant it would be to eventual economic pressures from new competitive entrants.

But Lumen's Revenues Are Expected To Remain Under Pressure

Lumen is yet to break its pattern of shrinking YoY revenue growth every quarter:

What's happening under the hood is that business (enterprise) revenues that Lumen is banking on is yet to see a meaningful revenue uptick, whilst the other mature and legacy business segments continue to see QoQ declines:

This headwind is expected to continue for at least 1–2 years, resulting in continued revenue pressure ahead:

I think it (legacy business) will continue to decline at rates that are a little faster than grow for the next year or 2.

- CFO Christopher Stansbury in the Wells Fargo TMT Summit Call.

The big uptick from PCF deals are expected to kick in and contribute $400-500 million in incremental recurring revenues only at the end of FY28:

Based on our current build schedule, the $10 billion of business in hand plus the existing O&M run rate business for PCF, we expect will yield a recurring revenue stream ranging between $400 million and $500 million by the time we exit 2028.

- CEO Kathleen Johnson in the Q3 FY25 earnings call.

Leverage Related Metrics Are A Key Risk Monitorable

Lumen is highly leveraged, with an annualized net debt/EBITDA position of 7.6x as of the latest quarter:

Now, a big catalyst for leverage reduction is the sale of a large part of Lumen's consumer fiber business for a cash consideration of $5.75 billion to AT&T. Subsequently, the company intends to pay down $4.8 billion of debt. The company expects this to bring down total leverage to 3.7-3.8x:

We'll use the proceeds from that sale, the after-tax proceeds as well as some cash on the balance sheet. And we will entirely shortly after closing, wipe out $4.8 billion of super priority debt, which is what we put in when we did the debt restructuring not quite 2 years ago. That will bring down our total debt from a little over $18 billion to just a little over $13 billion, bring our total leverage down to 3.7, 3.8.

- CFO Christopher Stansbury in the Wells Fargo TMT Summit Call.

However, my numbers, which include the impact of leases in the overall net debt calculation, imply a reduction down to 5.2x.

It is also important to realize that debt reduction is not the only thing that matters. This is because revenue pressures are also weighing down on EBITDA:

As a result, the EBITDA interest coverage ratios have also been declining over the past 3 years:

And the EBITDA expectations for the next 4 quarters are also being revised downward:

So if the company's financials plays out as per these expectations, there may be further interest coverage pressure.

Valuations Are Frothy

LUMN is currently trading at a 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA of 7.40x, which implies a 29.4% premium to its longer term median multiple of 5.72x. Looking at the dissection of the EV drivers into the 1-yr fwd EV/EBITDA multiple and the 1-yr fwd EBITDA expectations also shows us signs of some overhype as the stock's value increase has largely been driven by steep multiple expansion, even when the underlying EBITDA expectations have declined:

Hence, I believe LUMN may be trading at some frothy valuations right now.

LUMN Is Posting A Genuine Bullish Reversal But Momentum Is Slowing Down

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of LUMN vs SPX500

On the monthly relative charts of LUMN vs SPX500, the ratio charts depict a strong bullish reversal with increased volumes. Over the last few months, there has been a healthy bullish advance as well. However, this has failed to break a key monthly resistance level. Hence, I interpret the momentum to be slowing down as I expect more sideways price action going ahead.

Takeaway & Positioning

Overall, I think Lumen is a promising turnaround play that is seeing encouraging deal signing momentum in its business model shift that aims to capture data center networking infrastructure demand from hyperscalers. But the problem is that the revenue impact from these long-term deal wins won't kick in for another 2 years or so. And in the meantime, the business is facing strong revenue decline headwinds in its legacy business. The company is also highly levered, although the divestment of its consumer fiber business will probably ease this somewhat. However, I believe we should also keep a watchful eye on Lumen's interest coverage ratios, which may be impacted by revenue headwinds flowing down to the EBITDA line.

From a valuations' perspective, LUMN stock looks expensive and overhyped as the recent rally up has been driven largely by multiple expansion, which has offset declining EBITDA expectations. Technically, vs the S&P500, LUMN's impressive bullish momentum has come to a halt at a key monthly resistance level.

Overall, I rate the stock a 'Market Perform/Hold'.

How To Interpret Hunting Alpha's Ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: S&P 500: Rule Of 20 Likely To Remain Misleading In 2026
data.description:
Rule of 20

Generations of investors have been guided – effectively I might add – by the so-called standing Rule of 20 (which I will simply refer to as the rule hereafter). The rule simply suggests that when the sum of the S&P 500’s (SP500) price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio and the Consumer Price Index (CPI) inflation rate is close to ~20, it indicates fair valuation. A sum greater than 20 suggests the market is overvalued, while a sum less than 20 suggests it is undervalued. The rule is so simple and intuitive that it is almost self-evident and irrefutable. When inflation is higher, SP500 earnings should be more heavily discounted and thus should trade at a lower P/E, and vice versa.

As for the magical number of 20, my bet is that it is derived from historical statistics. As an example, the table below shows some historical observations following the rule (quoted from a WhittierTrust report). Following the above approach, readers can run back testing statistics to derive variations of the rule for other major market indices such as the DJI, NASDAQ, and/or RTY.

  • Markets rarely trade at equilibrium, so it’s no surprise that the Rule of 20 is also rarely achieved in precision.
  • The combined P/E ratio and inflation rate has ranged from a low of 14 to a high of 34.
  • Over the last 50 years or so, the average P/E has been just below 16, average inflation is 4% and the average sum of P/E and inflation, as expected, is close to 20.

Although if you have followed the rules in recent years, your investment results would be disappointing. Take 2025 as an example, SP500 traded at a P/E ratio of 28.16x in Jan 2025 per Multpl data. Also from the same source, inflation was 3% in Q1 2025. As such, the Rule of 20 sum was 28.16 + 3.0 = 31.16. This value is far higher than the 20 stated by the rule. It would thus strongly suggest the market is extremely overvalued, yet the market has continued to demonstrate resilience and deliver one of the best YTD return (of over 16%) as seen in the chart below. Repeating the same exercise for 2024 would also show misleading results – even more so than in 2025.

And next, I will argue why the rule will remain misleading into 2026 as well.

The role of interest rates

The most important and most fundamental weakness of the rule lies in its failure to account for the dramatically different macroeconomic environment of the modern era, particularly persistently lower interest rates. As aforementioned, the magic number of 20 was derived from past data, which featured far higher interest rates, as evidenced by the chart below. To wit, since the 1970’s, 10-year treasury rates have averaged 5.82%. The rates peaked at over 15% in the 1980s and have been on a secular decline since then. The current reading of 4.02% is considered “elevated” by many investors but it is quite mild in my view by historical standard. For example, it is noticeably below the long-term average of 5.82%.

Looking ahead, I feel confident to make a strong assertion that we won’t see treasury rates as high as those observed in the 1970~2000 again in my lifetime (and I’m in my mid-forties). Risk-free rates serve as gravity on all assets’ valuations. Thus, it is only natural for lower rates to be accompanied by higher P/E ratios for the equity market, thus invalidating the predicting power of the rule.

Out of the many reasons behind my above assertion, they all boil down to the budget balance of the US. As you all know, the debt of the country has expanded manyfold since the 1970’s as a fraction of our GDP. In the meantime, corporate tax rates have been drastically lowered over the years, as you can clearly see from the following chart. Based on data from the Peterson Foundation, due to the lower tax rates, the United States now collects fewer revenues from corporations, measured as a percentage of its GDP, than most similarly developed countries. In 2022, for example, the latest year for which data is available for international comparison, U.S. corporate tax revenues accounted for just 1.3 percent of GDP, while the average fraction is 3.2% among the 13 developed countries.

Given the combination of higher debt (either in absolute amount or as a percentage of GDP) and the reduced corporate tax income, I don’t expect the country to allow – or afford – borrowing rates to become as high as those we’ve seen in the 1970~2000 era again.

Other risks and final thoughts

Of course, the rule's weakened predicative power is not all due to negative developments in the US. For example, the next figure displays the profit margin of the SP500, with data also dating back to the 1970s. Despite all the random fluctuations, the trend has been overall upward. With the Q3 earnings data reported by October, the index’s average profit margin now sits at 10.9%. This is considerably above and almost double the median of 5.7% in the past 5 decades or so.

Out of the many forces that have been fueling this secular margin expansion, the globalization process we’ve witnessed – and enjoyed – since the 1970s is definitely the most important one on my list. As seen, global trade as a percentage of global GDP started at around 20% in the 1960s per World Bank Group data. The percentage kept rising and peaked at around 63% in 2022. Behind such a rise was the infrastructure invested over the years to facilitate a global network of supply chains, manufacturing facilities, transport equipment, communication systems, etc. I have no doubt that such infrastructure will keep benefiting the SP500 companies’ margins in the future, thus providing another support for the index to trade at a P/E higher than its historical averages.

On the downside, the ongoing trade disputes could be a place where my above projections could go wrong – at least in the near term. As you can see from the chart below, this globalization trend has been in reverse since its peak in 2022. Global trade as a percentage of global GDP has declined to 58% in 2023 and 57% in 2024. It might decline more in 2025 with new tariff rates between the US and its major trading partners.

To conclude, the thesis of this article is quite straightforward. My goal is to caution investors to NOT to rely on the Rule of 20 for 2026’s investment decisions. This will likely lead to a misleadingly bearish diagnosis of equity market conditions. The rule’s simplistic addition of P/E and inflation fails to capture the key market dynamics of the present day such as interest rates, role of global trades, and the equity market’s profitability metrics.

data.title: High-Beta Risk Remains Top-Performing Equity Factor In 2025
data.description:
As the trading year heads into its final weeks, so-called high-beta shares remain firmly in the lead for US equity risk factors in 2025, based on a set of ETFs through Tuesday’s close (Dec. 2).

The year-to-date performance for the Invesco S&P 500 High Beta ETF (SPHB) is still well ahead of the rest of the field and the stock market overall, based on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). As of yesterday’s close, SPHB’s total return is a sizzling 29.4% vs. SPY’s 17.3%.

SPHB’s bull run this year leaves the rest of the factor field in the dust. The next-best performer: large-cap growth (IVW), which is up 21.9%.

The key takeaway: high risk has paid off in 2025, by a wide margin. SPHB’s portfolio tracks an index that targets a subset of the S&P 500 based on the 100 stocks with the highest sensitivity to market movements, or beta, over the past 12 months via quarterly rebalancing.

Meanwhile, small-cap stocks remain laggards in the factor horse race this year. The weakest of the weak: small-cap growth (IJT), which is up a mild 5.4% year to date.

But hope springs eternal, still, for small caps. A Bloomberg report this week notes: “Traders are placing bullish bets on small-cap stocks even though they have struggled to outperform their larger cohort over the past year.”

Christopher Jacobson, Susquehanna International Group’s co-head of derivatives strategy, outlines the reasoning: “After getting hit in response to more hawkish rate cut expectations, the small caps have been a primary beneficiary of the more recent dovish tilt,” he wrote in a note to clients.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Circle Internet Group: Finally Undervalued For Its Growth
data.description:
Circle Internet Group (CRCL) is the second-largest tokenizer of USD stablecoins. With Q3 results out and a dip in the stock price over the past month, I wanted to look again, and I'm glad I did. I upgrade CRCL to Buy.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I wrote about Circle in September, I gave it a Hold rating, while realizing that the declining price was making it close to attractive.

Part of that attractiveness relied on the growth of its underlying stablecoin industry. To reiterate, Circle's business is tokenizing real currency into stablecoins. Their main product is their USD coin (OTC:USDC). Revenue derives from short-term interest rates on the cash reserve they hold. Circle is sufficiently capital-light that this alone can make for a profitable business. I could foresee a case of $2B to $3B in yearly earnings by 2030.

I did warn about the impact of the Fed's rate cuts, which would directly weaken the short-term yields on their cash reserves. I wrote:

For many quarters now, the short-term rates that their dollar reserves earn have been in decline. The Federal Reserve also finally issued a cut this September, so this is likely to continue. Lacking another way to make revenue, this will simply reduce earnings. Even if short-term rates don't return to zero, something like 2% could bring the range down between $1B and $1.5B. That could lower the valuation over time as well.

This is part of why I didn't rate the stock a Buy, instead preferring it to be more conservatively valued and under $120 before it would be worth buying.

Q3 Updates and Distress in Crypto

The third quarter marked another period of growth for the company.

Total USDC in circulation grew from $61.3B to $71.3B. A larger share of USDC was also attributed to their own platform.

This supported a 12.4% gain in revenue, quarter-over-quarter, while distribution costs only grew 10%.

Free cash flow for the three quarters has come to about $253M as well. Its first year as a public company is showing that it is highly cash-generative with its simple revenue model.

In spite of the good results, CRCL's share price fell below $100, the lowest it's been since the IPO earlier this year. This largely follows bearishness around Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and the impact this has had on several crypto-related stocks.

While stablecoins, as their name implies, do not crash alongside other cryptocurrencies, they are often used by crypto traders as a place to hold money between trades. This decline also coincides with the Federal Reserve's second rate cut, which occurred on October 29.

Thus, while things were looking good in Q3's results, the market has lately been worried about the broader ecosystem of crypto and how the rate cuts will weaken Circle's yield on its cash, which is substantially all of their revenue.

Outlook and Valuation

With these price declines, CRCL is about $78 per share, for a market cap of about $18B. I think this is good value and finally makes the stock a Buy. Let's take a look at why.

First, the market cap of USDC (effectively, the total cash pile) did decline with the broader crypto market in November but recovered and reached new highs as the month ended. Even the low point of November was comparable to where it was at the end of Q3. USDC is still growing, and therefore Circle's business is growing.

The company reported $214.3M in net income for Q3. If we adjust for portions that don't reflect their earnings model (removing depreciation, digital asset gains, and their tax benefit) that gets us $174.4M in earnings, which annualizes to $697.6M. If this amount grows at a 17% CAGR by 2030, then CRCL's current market cap is just a P/E of 10 for that moment.

Some may doubt their ability to grow their base of USD. USDC is second place to Tether's USD coin (USDT-USD). This was also asked during Q&A in Q3 earnings call, and I thought CEO Jeremy Allaire had a good answer to that.

And I think the misunderstanding is that stablecoin networks are like other Internet platform utilities, meaning they have network effects...We see major B2B firms adding USDC as the payment option without a deal with Circle, without any relationship with Circle, they're doing it because it's got the most reach and interoperability.

I think this speaks largely to the fact that Circle, being a public company, has completed all the requirements in auditing and transparency. This is important because stablecoin minters need to show that they keep the reserve currency at a 1:1 ratio, as opposed to investing the cash in higher-yield assets. Otherwise, a scenario similar to a bank run could occur, leading to the failure of the coin.

Tether promised to have a public audit by one of the big four auditors in March, but to date no such audit has been announced and released. Circle therefore has a trust advantage that should allow it to grow and gain entirely new business among financial institutions and vendors that want a reliable token for USD.

With the next FOMC meeting days away, another rate cut is possible and seen as increasingly likely. Single meeting won't change much, and what really matters is what happens to rates over a series of FOMC meetings. If rates eventually decline to 2%, then USDC would need about twice as much market cap to support current revenues and earnings.

Citi (C) offered a promising report on the growth of stablecoins. They made the following observation:

Bank tokens, with characteristics such as trust and privacy, will remain preferred by many. We believe 2030 transaction volumes for bank tokens may exceed stablecoins.

All Circle needs to do is form a similar distributor relationship with them as it has with Coinbase (COIN).

Even the bear case of the report suggests more than a 3X of the stablecoin market by 2030. If Circle simply maintains its usual market share, there's the earnings growth even with 2% rates. As the biggest player with fully audited reserves, I think they can at least do this.

Perhaps the risk factor that could deal the biggest blow to earnings would be a Fed that cuts rates aggressively. President Trump has been vocal about his desire for rate cuts, and with Jerome Powell's term as Chairman expiring in May, his replacement is likely to be one that Trump sees as aligning with his political goals. This means rate cuts faster than the growth of USDC's market cap could occur, hurting Circle's earnings for the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

I think CRCL has worked very hard to create a reliable system and is in a position to scale and expand stablecoins into use cases beyond crypto markets, into more traditional finance. They may not lead in size alone, but they lead in trust, and with the secular tailwinds, even in a more bearish case, it would take several setbacks working together for an investment in CRCL to disappoint. That is why I upgrade my rating to Buy today.

data.title: Paramount Resources: Cash Rolls In Just When It Is Needed
data.description:
As previous articles noted, Paramount Resources (OTCPK:PRMRF) sold most of its production and has spent much of this year restarting the business. In the company presentation, there are plans to get back to where the business was before the sale by the end of 2027. But any long-term plans in this industry are usually highly dependent upon commodity prices. However, this management has been around the block a few times and therefore is taking out insurance policies. (Remember this is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.) The first "extra" was the sale of NuVista (OTCPK:NUVSF) shares that meant receipts of nearly C$300 million. Now, Mother Nature appears to be aiding the cause with an unusually early breakdown of the polar vortex that threatens to send unusually cold air South to strengthen natural gas prices.

Last Article

The last article mentioned the outperformance of a brand-new gas plant thanks to some unexpected well performances that were boosting volumes beyond the original forecast.

Those extra volumes may well receive some of the best natural gas prices in some years thanks to a breakdown of the Polar Vortex noted above. There is absolutely nothing that beats luck in this business. This management has more luck than anyone has a right to expect, and that luck is happening at precisely the right time.

Cash received now is nearly always more valuable than cash received later because it can be invested in the rebuilding project to increase production volumes sooner.

This is making that sale of the production a better prospect "by the minute" because building the business back sooner rather than later increases the future potential cash flow.

Right now, this is a financially strong company with a lot of cash and no long-term debt. But what is now underway could have been much worse than what is actually happening. The whole situation could mean very good things for the stock price sooner rather than later.

Production Guidance Change

The last article also covered the following production guidance change:

What the production revision does is potentially generate more cash that affects the cash burn rate for the project underway. This could later show itself in the form of a higher final level of production when the project completes. It could also possibly become a dividend raise sooner rather than later. Let us see which way management chooses to go with this.

Meanwhile, the sale of NuVista stock helps to make sure that the company gets where it is going if commodity prices turn out to be less than the original forecast. The evidence that the rebuilding project will get done as planned under a wider range of industry conditions (than was originally the case) is piling up very fast.

There is also the possibility that, if the well outperformance persists as more wells come online, then there may be a lower budget needed for the company to achieve the volumes originally planned.

The thing to remember about volume outperformance is the infrastructure needs to be in place for processing (meaning gas plant capacity) and for transportation (supporting pipeline infrastructure). If there is any material volume revision upwards, some of the capital budget savings may need to be redirected towards more supporting infrastructure in at least those areas.

Overall, this is a very cheerful predicament to be in. This is one company that is cheering on the cold air.

Alhambra Plant

This is where the overproduction is taking place that caused the increase in the production guidance. Now whether what is happening here can spread to other acreage is an open question because technology seems to be advancing at a good pace. Even if that does happen, different acreage can benefit from whatever advances caused that overproduction at a likely different pace than was the original case.

Very likely the plant expansion will not be accelerated even though production is coming in higher than expected. The reason for this is that a fair amount of the materials used have to be ordered in advance. So, there is likely little that can be done with immediate plans.

However, the next expansion project can be adjusted as needed should management feel the need to do so. This was always going to be a three-part expansion. Whether a fourth expansion will be needed or a lower drilling budget is something we will find out as this project progresses.

But the immediate change due to the production increase is likely to be a lower-than-budgeted cash burn rate. After that, it will be the most profitable alternative given the lower cash burn rate.

Summary

This is a company that has raised a fair amount of money, and now Mother Nature may raise some more money at a time that the company could really use that cash on the balance sheet. The very cold winter is highly likely to raise the price of natural gas beyond the usual winter expectations. That would likewise provide some extra cash flow to raise the cash balance beyond planning expectations.

In an industry that is notoriously volatile and low visibility, the more cash available, the better. It lessens the risk of long-term borrowing when this particular project nears the end.

Paramount Resources remains a strong buy idea with what is likely to remain a very strong balance sheet with little or no debt. The current dividend level seems very secure to the point that there may well be a discussion about raising that dividend depending upon the cash build.

The well outperformance also points to some potential material savings. This depends upon whether or not that outperformance continues on the acreage where it began and whether or not something from that outperformance can be used elsewhere to either raise initial well production or reduce capital expenditures.

But when it comes to luck, things certainly seem to be going management's way early in this major project. What needs to be seen is the extent to which the initial trends continue.

Risks

La Ninas are actually warm without the cold air leaks to the South that they are known for. Right now, there is a very clear cold air leak going on. But there is also a long way for winter to go. If there are no more leaks after this one, the rest of the winter could prove to be unseasonably warm enough to offset what is going on now. Such a scenario is not likely precisely because La Ninas are known for bouts of cold (that give La Ninas their cold reputation). But it certainly is possible.

Any major project can have unforeseen challenges that lead to cost overruns, delays, and other unpleasant outcomes. The current well outperformance that has the market excited could not only end, but it also could be followed by a series of underperforming wells.

There is political uncertainty marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs. This has the market looking for a recession. That could eventually bring on a period of weak commodity prices that are not currently envisioned. It actually makes the current spate of cash raises and the cold winter even more important. A sustained recession or worse could delay or even abort the planned growth.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DaVita: More Volatile But Better Upside Than Fresenius
data.description:
I've been covering DaVita (DVA) as a secondary ticker for some time, next to my coverage of Fresenius Medical Care (FMS). It's fair to say that DVA is a peer to Fresenius, but one with a lower rate of overall credit safety, a lower dividend, and more earnings volatility than FMS.

I haven't covered DaVita as a primary ticker before. It's interesting because just because Fresenius Medical Care is attractive here, it does not make DaVita unattractive in turn. DaVita is, in fact, a very volatile business, with significant ups and downs in relatively short periods of time. This makes it a potentially attractive value investment if we can target the prices where it's attractive and where it is not.

In this article, I'll be establishing my coverage on DaVita. The company is relatively undercovered on Seeking Alpha. The last article is now over 3 months old. What can be said is that over the last year, while the market has seen growth of over 10%, DaVita has seen a decline of more than 27%. This is despite ongoing share buybacks. In this market, this is a noteworthy development and potentially indicative of significant undervaluation. At least, this could be the case if the company's fundamentals are solid.

So that's what we'll focus on here.

It's also obvious that the market feels like there's an issue with DaVita here, that should prevent us from investing in the business. To be safe, we'll look at this as well and determine how much we should be listening to or considering this.

What can be said is that Quant and Wall Street analytics have the company at a "HOLD" rating despite a valuation grade of A-, owing to issues with company growth rates, recent momentum, and revisions.

Let's see what this company has to offer.

DaVita - Upside and fundamentals, at the price of risk and volatility

DaVita is a provider of dialysis products and services. On that level, it directly competes with peers like Fresenius Medical Care. However, the company has been through numerous issues, the most central of which being earnings dropping year over year. These drops in core KPIs have occurred despite the company repurchasing a significant number of shares. It takes quite a bit of negative news for a company to continue to decline despite share repurchases.

The company's services have the same end-users as Fresenius, looking at patients with chronic ESRD or kidney failure conditions. Much like Fresenius, it operates outpatient kidney service centers and also offers lab services. The company is US-centric, with a fair bit of international exposure. With over 30 years of experience, the company is far from a newcomer on the scene. It's one of the leading companies in its field, with over 2,500 dialysis centers.

Its business operations are, as a consequence, heavily reliant on the medical reimbursement/healthcare system. Much of its sales of services is directly tied to Medicare and Medicaid programs, despite its focus on research as well as direct care.

I would argue the company, despite some of the uncertainties in its fundamentals, has a narrow moat in dialysis services, as it operates over a third of all dialysis clinics in the US. In the context of US operations, its international operations can be viewed as almost negligible, because it's the US business that will drive success or failure. Its network of clinics across the states is not easily replicable, and even in the case of policy or reimbursement changes, its services will be crucial and necessary. In addition, many of DaVita's clinics are staked by referring physicians, which adds to the ecosystem and connection that's being run here.

This can also be called a bit of a risk to the company, given DoJ settlements, but this is the state of things at this time.

The company also serves over three-quarters of the US market and therefore enjoys significant scale advantages compared to other companies. What the company does is, rightly, in the US, called a "mature" sort of market. Between FMS and DVA, there seems to be very little need for the addition of further clinics, as increased demand can likely be handled by existing infrastructure. As an argument against the argument made above with regard to being run in part by its physicians, it's also worth mentioning that this sort of service is subject to significant regulatory scrutiny.

Regulatory scrutiny means, for one, that there's some commoditization (DVA can't charge what it wants; the government checks on this). For instance, for over 30 months after the start of dialysis, commercial insurance plans are primary payors for the services, and traditionally, this is when DaVita (and other) clinics make much of their profit. If the patient then needs the service after 33 months, much lower, regulated Medicare rates get activated. These are often close to break-even levels for these clinics.

This means both positives and negatives for a company like DaVita - the low long-term returns likely mean a discouragement for new entrants, and pricing is not a factor (we're talking an inelastic as opposed to elastic pricing model), but it also means that from a DVA financial perspective, the ideal patient is one that is cured within 30 months - which is rarely the case for ESRD/kidney failure.

The low number of new companies in the space for the past 10-20 years that have achieved any sort of scale implies that DaVita is an efficient operator, much like other existing ones.

Fundamentally speaking, the main problem is the non-existent yield, which means that the entirety of your profits is dependent on capital appreciation, the BB-credit, which is considered junk-rated, and the company's unimpressive RoR since 2021.

The company has been forecasting its tipping point and upside for several years now. It was estimated that 2021-2022 would result in rapid growth with significantly higher results in 2023-2024. That did not happen in the way forecasted, and for the current 2025E periods, it's expected for the company to grow in earnings lower than 10%. This is not bad, but the requirements for a junk-rated, no-dividend company need to be set higher than a dividend-paying company with different return metrics.

Aside from forecast failure, the primary risk ascribed to DaVita is its obvious dependence on Medicare reimbursements. With at-home services expanding, the returns here are more uncertain because this could open the market up to new entrants. Political risks are significant, with the US Congress set to reduce US healthcare spending - Medicare could be part of those cuts, and these effects would be direct.

I'm not the only analyst to consider Davita's 3Q25 results as negative, with the business clearly missing analyst expectations for not only EPS but for revenue as well. The market reacted accordingly. Most of this was related to lower treatment volumes in dialysis, its core operation, as well as a security incident this year.

Moving on to valuation for DaVita.

Valuation for DaVita - Upside is there, but it comes at significant risk

To put it simply, the market has now devalued the company enough, despite its somewhat speculative characteristics. Trading at less than 11.5x P/E, it may not be as low as Fresenius Medical Care was trading when it was at record lows, but it's low enough when we consider that DaVita often has beaten estimates, as well as how high the current growth rates are expected to be.

The company's leverage, coupled with the market risk, makes this investment of a speculative nature, but the returns offered here are compelling enough that I would believe this risk to be somewhat offset by the returns offered here, even sans a yield.

DaVita does not need to outperform here. It does not even need to hit its 20-year or 5-year average to give you returns in excess of the market. It just needs to hit the lowest multiple that it's been able to trade at, on average, for the past 20 years. When we put this into the context of current forecasts, we can ask ourselves if the risk of such a development is high or low. I personally view the risk as fair enough that the company will be able to generate such returns as to trade, over time, at 13-14x P/E. I back this up using the company's historical average chance to hit or beat its targets.

It's also worth noting that if the company does outperform its stated targets or expectations and manages to hit more of a 20-year average P/E, which is completely valid based on historical accuracy, then this is the rate of return you would be looking at

The reason I chose to write about DaVita now is that the company is in a position to generate triple-digit RoR on the basis of a conservative 20-year P/E average. This is significant on several levels, but it's especially significant in the market we have today, where much of the market is overvalued to a high degree.

Based on this, I give the company a conservative fair value target of $185/share, with a PT of $140/share. The relative PT is so low because of the volatility and complete lack, and complete unlikelihood given the debt, of a (future) dividend. I believe that I'm being fair with regard to both the ups and downs here.

Alternatives are clear to me - you could buy FMS - but DVA has a higher upside. If your risk appetite allows you to purchase this stock and hold it for upside through even a market decline, then that could be a good idea for you.

Risks are as follows.

Risks and Considerations

The risks for DaVita relative to its forecast are primarily related to Medicare reimbursement. Most of the other risk factors fade, in my view, when the company's relative business volume and the risks a cut would entail are put in context. In contrast to other analysts, I consider the risk for revolutionary new drugs or treatments (including things like more regenerative medicine) as something speculative, and not something to directly impact the valuation.

Even with the coming and integration of at-home care, this would hardly negate the company's advantage in terms of intangible assets and its center network, though it may certainly impact its profitability.

These are the primary risks I see to my positive, if somewhat more speculative, rating on DaVita.

Thesis

DaVita is a potentially attractive company. The company's fundamentals, including its credit rating, lack of a yield, and historical volatility, represent an investment hurdle requiring careful consideration as to how we should set the bar for valuation for the company.

For myself, I elect to set the bar at the lowest 20-year average available for the company, which comes to a 13x P/E. At 13x P/E and a double-digit rate of growth, we can see a fair value for the company coming to 20-25% per year, or a total RoR of over 55%+ until fiscal 2027E.

Being that this is a conservative thesis, I give the company a conservative fair value of $185/share, with a PT of about $130/share. That makes the company a "BUY". It should be noted, however, that there's a fair bit of volatility here, and this company is therefore more speculative than not.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company, if a speculative one. Based on this and a price target of $140/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of December 2025.

data.title: ChargePoint Q3 Earnings Preview: The Pain Is Far From Over
data.description:
I can take dozens of examples from the history of corporate America to drive home the point that the growth of a particular industry does not automatically guarantee the success of all industry participants. The auto industry's evolution since 1920 is a classic example of this. Today, I believe we are seeing this in the EV sector. The adoption of EVs is likely to remain strong for many years to come.

However, not all companies in this sector will succeed. I turned bearish on ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) in September 2023 when the stock was trading at a split-adjusted price of $115. Since then, CHPT stock has lost more than 90% of its value as my bearish thesis played out to perfection. I raised a few concerns in my September 2023 article and also in another article published in January 2024.

  1. I argued that ChargePoint's failure to convert strong revenue growth into profits was a warning sign and that it could deteriorate investor sentiment toward CHPT if growth slows down.
  2. I believed that Tesla's NACS would pose an existential threat to ChargePoint as the opening of Tesla's Supercharger network to all automakers would result in a massive network disadvantage for ChargePoint.
  3. I was wary of an inventory glut that could stem from the waning demand for EVs at the time.
  4. I was not a huge fan of ChargePoint management's cost reduction efforts as I thought such efforts would not lead to sustainable earnings. I discussed why the management's EBITDA goal was likely to fall short.

In hindsight, all of these concerns have actually dented ChargePoint's financials and its stock market performance in the past 2 years.

With ChargePoint expected to report FQ3 2026 results after the bell tomorrow (December 4), I thought of revisiting my thesis to determine if CHPT is finally investable on the back of a disappointing performance since 2023.

The FQ3 Earnings Print Will Reveal ChargePoint's Business Transformation Progress

ChargePoint, when I turned bearish, was still a fast-growth company. However, things have dramatically changed for the worse in the past couple of years. The company has reported YoY revenue growth declines in each of the past 8 quarters. As illustrated below, revenue has fallen off a cliff.

Exhibit 1: ChargePoint revenue

For the third quarter of Fiscal 2026, analysts estimate revenue of ~$96 million. The company is expected to report a loss of $1.31 per share. Moving beyond headline numbers, I am focusing on a few key metrics within the Q3 earnings report to evaluate ChargePoint's true business health.

First, Q3 numbers will help us determine if the low cash burn (~$2 million) reported by the company in the previous quarter is sustainable or not. The EBITDA positive target has already been stretched from 2024 to 2026. A sudden spike in cash burn in Q3 should be considered as a warning sign of a further delay in ChargePoint's positive EBITDA goal. I have already identified some complexities facing ChargePoint's reported cash burn which I will share in the next segment of this analysis.

Second, I will be keeping a close eye on the hardware business performance in Q3. In FQ2, hardware revenue (networked charging systems) declined 21% YoY to $50.4 million. Continued struggles in this business segment are likely to have a major impact on the subscription business in the coming quarters (more on this later). The key here is understanding the real drivers behind the decline in hardware revenue. In Q2, the management attributed the decline in hardware revenue to a few external factors, including the wait-and-see approach by potential customers amid tax credit expirations, slowing EV sales growth, and delays for secured projects.

Third, I will be paying attention to the management's guidance for FQ4 to determine if external conditions have improved according to the management's view. If revenue guidance falls below $100 million (for the current quarter, the management has guided for revenue of $90-$100 million) once again, I would assume that external challenges are continuing to impact ChargePoint's business. To be honest, I believe the post-earnings market reaction will be dictated by the management guidance for the upcoming quarter.

The Hardware Sales Slowdown Is Worse Than It Seems

Many ChargePoint bulls seem to be focused on the high-margin subscriptions business of the company. In FQ2, subscription revenue increased ~10% YoY to $39.89 million. This made it look like the subscriptions business was immune to the decline in hardware revenue, but in reality, this is far from the truth. There is often a lag between hardware revenue and software revenue. However, these two businesses are interconnected given that software revenue largely depends on the expansion of the installed base. The decline in hardware revenue in FQ2 will only be felt by the subscription business a few quarters later because of this lag. The ~10% growth in subscriptions revenue reported in FQ2 masks the challenges that lie ahead for this business segment in 2026.

There are 2 main reasons to suggest that hardware sales will continue to remain under pressure in the foreseeable future.

First, EV sales are likely to lose some momentum in the coming quarters as tax credits are no longer boosting the demand for EVs. For ChargePoint, the biggest blow comes from the repeal of the up to $40,000 in commercial clean vehicle tax credits effective from September 30. For ChargePoint, fleet electrification was meant to be a big driver of revenue as fleet owners were expected to install EV chargers on site to support their fleet transformation efforts. With the total cost of ownership of a commercial EV now expected to rise in the absence of tax credits, I believe we are looking at the possibility of further project delays and even the cancellation of committed charging infrastructure orders.

Even at a broad level, EV sales are likely to take a massive hit as a result of the Trump administration's decision to repeal EV tax credits ($7,500 for new EVs and $4,500 for used EVs). According to research by Princeton University, the repeal of tax credits and EPA emissions regulations could result in a 30% decline in projected EV growth in 2027 alone.

Exhibit 2: Expected slowdown in EV sales

Given that we are looking at a slowdown in EV adoption, I believe charging infrastructure investments will also slowdown in the coming quarters. This will keep ChargePoint's hardware revenue under pressure in the foreseeable future.

Second, ChargePoint is facing a massive competitive threat from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). In the wake of NACS, ChargePoint was quick to modify its ports to support NACS but the threat does not end here. Tesla wants to be the undisputed leader in EV charging infrastructure as well. This has resulted in the company aggressively investing in expanding its fast-charging network. According to Paren, in Q3, Tesla added 1,820 new fast-charging ports in the U.S., accounting for almost 45% of total new port additions during the quarter. ChargePoint added only 300 new ports.

While the industry as a whole is suffering from a slowdown in investments altogether, Tesla is strategically using this challenging time period to establish its dominance in charging infrastructure. This is not good news for ChargePoint.

Overall, I believe the hardware revenue slowdown in Q2 will extend for a few quarters, eventually impacting subscription revenue growth as well.

The Low Cash Burn In Q2 Is Deceptive

One of the most promising data points in the Q2 earnings print was ChargePoint's very low cash burn. Commenting on this seemingly positive development, CFO Mansi Khetani said:

Speaking of cash, we ended the quarter with $195 million of cash on hand versus $196 million in the prior quarter, resulting in cash usage of less than $2 million. This compares with $49 million of cash usage in Q2 of last year and $29 million in Q1 this year. We have been able to significantly reduce cash burn over the past few quarters, mainly due to spend reductions and working capital management.

A closer look at the company's cash usage and accounting policies suggests that this ultra-low cash burn in Q2 did not come on the back of a major increase in operating cash flows, nor from a major improvement in operating efficiency. While ChargePoint has indeed improved its operating efficiency in the recent past, working capital management had a lot to do with ChargePoint's low cash burn in Q2.

  • In Q2, accounts payable increased by $8.4 million (if the management decided to keep accounts payable flat compared to the previous quarter, it would have increased cash burn by $8.4 million).
  • Accrued liabilities increased by $7.7 million in Q2.

If both these line items remained flat, the company would have burned just over $18 million in cash in Q2. A normalization in working capital trends will see the company burning much more than just $2 million in cash in the coming quarters.

Takeaway

ChargePoint is expected to report FQ3 earnings on December 4. I am looking forward to the management guidance for FQ4 to determine if macro conditions are improving. For now, findings of my research suggest ChargePoint's hardware revenue growth is likely to remain depressed in the foreseeable future, posing a threat to subscription revenue growth in the coming quarters. I continue to believe that CHPT is uninvestable for now with no major catalysts on the horizon to hint at a possible turnaround in prospects.

data.title: Is Your Retirement Income Enough? How I Leveled Up Mine With Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
The harsh reality is that most asset prices are negatively correlated with the changes in interest rates. For example, if the rates go down, the asset prices go up. The mathematical consequence of this is that the yields tend to fall as the denominator in the 'dividend per share to share price' formula increases.

Given that the Fed has already made several cuts from the peak interest rate level in mid-2024, we should not be surprised that the overall yield levels for most assets have gone down accordingly. Be it T-bills (TBIL), real estate (VNQ), high-yield bonds (HYG), infrastructure (BIP), telecommunication (IYZ) etc., almost everywhere we look, the yields are indeed lower than, say, at the start of 2024.

When it comes to the immediate future, then there is nothing positive to say in relation to enjoying attractive yields. Namely, the chances are very high that soon (December 2025 or early 2026) the Fed would announce incremental base rate cuts, putting an additional pressure on the asset yields.

All of this is not good news for investors, who have the following objectives:

  • Retire or live off from passive income.
  • Grow the income-producing asset base to hit the envisaged income figure.
  • Take balanced risk, which would reduce income volatility and, in general, warrant better sleep at night.
  • Avoid portfolio asset sales to fund consumption needs.

In other words, investors, who are focused on building up a significant passive income machine without taking too aggressive risks have now (and will have going forward) a more difficult job at hand. And by difficult I mean achieving the necessary target income level by a certain date or in as capital efficient way as possible.

If I look deeper into my portfolio, then apart from BDCs (BIZD), MLPs (AMLP) and leveraged closed end funds (PDI), I have to say that the reinvestment yields have become much less attractive than before. While in early 2024 I could easily find assets that, on average, provided safe 7% to 9% yields, then now I have to say the amplitude has moved down into 5% to 7% territory.

For example, in just over the past 6 month period, British American Tobacco (BTI) yield has decreased by 21%, for Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (NYSE:PINE) by 9%, for Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG) by 6%, for Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB) by 6% etc. The only areas where the yields have somewhat increased in my portfolio are where I have already mentally factored in some income reductions in the next 1 - 3 year period - more specifically, the BDC and leveraged CEF space.

Long story short, the current situation for income investors is worse than it was not that long ago and going forward it will very likely become even more difficult (time consuming) to snowball passive income flows.

Now, luckily, we (and I) have the covered call ETF opportunity (segment) to explore and integrate in our income portfolios to fight back the yield-pressures coming from more traditional income-oriented assets.

The way how I approach covered call ETFs

If we think about the covered call ETF pay-off profiles, then I would say there are some fundamental similarities to high-yield credit.

Just think of it.

The pay-off for high-yield credit consists of two components:

  • Getting back principal or slightly profiting from its increase if, say, the high-yield issuer delivers robust financials, experiences positive credit migration etc.
  • Pocketing relatively attractive coupon, which usually explains the lion's share of total return that is associated with this high-yield credit investment.

At the same time, we we are assuming fully open downside risk where we could end up losing the entire principal (issuer default) and relatively risky coupons (could be delayed, reduced after potential restructuring etc.).

Now, if we look at a standard covered call ETF - JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) -, which writes out of the money call options on a certain underlying index, then basically the pay-off profile becomes quite similar to the high-yield case:

  • Potentially profiting from slight price appreciation that would come from the index increasing in price until the capped ceiling (from call option sale) is reached.
  • Capturing very attractive but inherently volatile dividends, which tend to also constitute majority of the total return figure.

Speaking of risks, there is also almost fully open downside risk that could materialize from plunging index prices, where the dividends in such case would also quickly disappear.

For this reason (bond-like pay-off profile) and the fact that I already have a decent capital deployed in PIMCO CEFs as well as BDCs, I have imposed a rather tight limit on the maximum covered call ETF exposure in my overall portfolio. The maximum is 15%, and, currently, the exposure is around 12%.

Having said that, since the average yield on my covered call ETF bucket is 13%, then still, I get a quite decent bang for the buck in the context of alternative ~7% yielding assets. To quickly put things into perspective, I would need 85% of additional capital into 7% yielding securities to arrive at the same level of income that my covered call ETFs generate.

However, I see that there are at least three important steps how we could optimize our covered call ETF exposures from the risk management and, ultimately, durable income generation perspective.

First, avoid covered call ETFs, which sell call options against a single company instead of a broadly diversified index or asset class, where the downside volatility is much less pronounced. YieldMax is quite active here with such vehicles as YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY) and YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY). Both of these funds have experienced significant NAV erosion and steep distribution cuts. Granted, if we add back the distributions made, the total return figures remain somewhat neutral (albeit, it depends on what time period we choose), but it does not change the fact that the risk level is too high for any prudent income investor.

Second, avoid building up overly concentrated covered call ETF positions in the typical indices - the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ). This is not easy as the most well-know covered call ETF funds such as JEPQ, the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX), the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) are linked to exactly SPY or QQQ. The issue is that, at least in my view, these indices are overvalued and in the case of AI-driven correction would almost certainly plunge. So, the combination of that happening and having most of portfolio covered call ETFs tied to tech-exposed areas would not be the greatest event to experience.

Here, I see a significant merit of including covered call ETFs (on top of some allocation in the AI-linked names) that are based on less correlated indices. My personal top picks are Neos Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) for real estate factor and the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) for gold factor. It is clear that in the case of armageddon event (system-wide sell-off), the asset price correlations converge to 1, thus weakening the argument or benefit of such diversification. However, not all sell-off are system-wide and, most likely, if the AI bet starts to lose steam, then I would easily see a case for the QQQ dropping at much larger rate than, say, real estate or gold.

Third, avoid building significant concentration into zero day option expiry covered call ETFs such as the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE) and the Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE). While these funds generate 30%+ dividend yields, they come at an elevated risk of suffering from a permanent impairment of NAV in a scenario where the market turns deeply south in a single-day period. The issue is that they lock in gains (capped gains) or losses (100% downside adjusted for option premiums) that have occurred in a single day and then in the following day write new call option contracts against the updated underlying value. If the downside path for the market is gradual, then XDTE and QDTE could outperform. Conversely, if the market experiences a sharp correction within one trading day and then recovers in the next day, XDTE and QDTE would not be able to benefit from the recovery, and, instead, be left with structurally impaired value base from which the forthcoming dividends would be lower accordingly.

The bottom line

Covered call ETFs are powerful tools for portfolio yield enhancement and income diversification in a falling interest rate environment, where many traditional income sources become less and less attractive.

At the same time, we have to be cognizant of risks that come with these high-yielding covered call ETF structures; which is only logical given the double digit dividends.

The key is to keep the covered call ETF exposure balanced (even though I fully understand the tendency to really overweight these ETFs due to their attractive yields) and to calibrate covered call portfolio in a manner that maximizes risk and return factor diversification in a prudent manner.

data.title: AI Won't Kill Adobe, Here's Why
data.description:
The argument against Adobe

This is the second time I covered Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE), the first time around was not successful with the shares dropping even further by -25%. This is one of those strange situations where the numbers continue to improve, yet Wall St. is betting that new technologies in artificial intelligence will eventually destroy Adobe's business or make similar services so cheap that price competition will ensue.

There's no doubt that new AI photo editing systems like Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) nano banana, mid journey, Dall E and others will eventually have an easily accessible app and that individuals that do a lot of multi-media editing will discover where to find it. For now, Adobe's numbers speak for themselves. These enterprise software systems are sticky and hard for companies to detach from once they subscribe to the product and have an ongoing workflow that their workforce uses such apps for everyday.

While I personally agree that what Google, OpenAI and others have done with instant picture alterations is amazing, it is not all that easy to operate for those that are not used to working with LLM systems.

LLMs will be engines rather than re-create every platform

Anti trust laws exist for a reason and the companies with those killer AI apps like Google, Open AI and Anthropic, would be best served to simply offer their models as engines rather than try to eat everyone else's lunch. They can still monetize their products without much scrutiny in this fashion while still allowing users to perform similar functions to popular software services through their Swiss army knife like chat apps. The minute they begin to create more and more stand alone apps or acquire companies in SAAS spaces that already have users bases is a risk that might not be worth taking.

I brought up a similar idea when looking at Docusign (DOCU), which I also still believe is incredibly cheap. The company had just had a sell off because ChatGPT created an "agent" that could analyze legal contracts in similar fashion to Docusign. However, if you read through Docusign's last investor presentation, you also realize that they themselves are woking with OpenAI to introduce such functions into their already familiar user interface.

This is also the crux of the argument, individuals are already very accustomed to the UI's of an a Adobe or a Docusign. A user would in my opinion much rather have an improved experience [incorporating these technologies into the UI behind the scenes], rather than learn something new.

LLMs are not all that intuitive for many

For those that type well and express themselves quickly, LLMs are a dream come true. I've personally run into many individuals who either hate typing or have fear of communicating with artificial intelligence for personal reasons. They are used to clicking buttons or dragging and dropping versus asking for something via their own language and creativity. I still believe that the button/drag and drop UI experience is going to remain for quite some time and take a while before individuals adapt to chatting with AI systems.

Year to date performance

Adobe has been one of the shockers of the market this year as several SAAS companies have sold off on the assumption that AI will eventually replace their businesses.

Off all time high

From an all-time high perspective, Adobe has more than been cut in half from it's record high. Any time you get a crack at a premier software company more than -50% off, you need to take a hard look at the current and forward fundamentals.

One thing we notice here is that from a current and forward GAAP and Non-GAAP multiples, the numbers are pretty static, indicating a very tepid expected growth rate in the next 12 months to come. Some analysts remain more optimistic about growth rates than the numbers on screeners would lead you to believe. I am more optimistic as well.

Numbers don't lie

If we use reflexive patterns and simply compare the 10 year growth rates in earnings per share [EPS], free cash flow and revenue to price, they are now all in excess of the price appreciation over this period. Very bullish from a value perspective if any kind of forward growth is left in the tank in years to come. This chart indicates investors are betting on a contraction in those growth rates.

Historical GAAP P/E ratios

Where do we sit in respect to history on a GAAP P/E ratio basis? At a trailing 20.07 X, we can visually see this is a level not seen since the last big financial crisis circa 2012.

Adobe offerings and revenue exposure

Adobe is not sitting on it's hands when it comes to AI. They are proactively teaming up with LLM partners to improve all of their platforms. We can see from the above that ecosystem partners include both Google and OpenAI. It's true in my opinion that there will only be a handful of companies that can create useful LLMs due to having the capital to own the cloud computing data center infrastructure. We can already see they are leveraging that power more as partners than as adversaries to existing SAAS platforms.

In Adobe's most recent presentation they also mention plug ins in beta for ChatGPT, Copilot, TikTok, Linkedin and others. If you try to replicate some Adobe functions in an LLM like ChatGPT, the result is often clunky and nowhere near as good as what you could get on Adobe. Some of these LLMs may find it useful to simply cut to the front of the line, design a partnered plug in and now allow users to use these functions through the LLMs and be part of your LLM subscription. SAAS companies in short can also help LLMs improve and if they so choose to form these partnerships, then it would be very difficult from a legal perspective to make a full on competitive product in the future for an AI LLM company.

Adobe is still showing Y/Y growth in subscriber revenue despite the negative outlook by the market. While you could say they are just milking the existing user base with new add-ons, Adobe is also reporting a 25% growth in monthly active users [up to 700 million plus]. Additionally, they are reporting 30% growth Q/Q for their AI app Firefly. Firefly is the text to image and image generation feature similar to what ChatGPT's Dall E3 would do or Mid Journey.

So again, using Firefly as an example, they may be using ChatGPT as an engine but through their own custom user interface that users are already used to, hence growth on Adobe's platform remains even though you could go out and piece together similar functions elsewhere.

Fair price on a PEG ratio basis

Here I'll take a look at a PEG ratio analysis using bullish high end estimates for next year [26] and ending FY 2025 on the average EPS estimate of $20.8 as our starting point. The PEG ratio again divides the multiple by the growth rate less the percent sign. A ratio of 1 or less is a heck of a deal. We can pin down this price by taking the predicted growth rate as the multiplier times the predicted EPS as the multiplicand.

Here are the assumptions:

  • FY 2025: $20.8
  • FY 2026: $24.28
  • Growth rate : 16.73%
  • PEG 1 equivalent : 16.73 X $24.28 = $414.9
  • Selling at 78% of intrinsic value as of 12/2/2025

Let's see how this compares to Wall St. Ratings:

Again, this target is not wild or out to left field and actually sits below the average target for Wall St. analysts. I believe a price on Adobe in the low to mid $400s sits well with in the realm of logic based on the numbers.

Risks

The very risk that Wall St. is anticipating is the main risk. LLMs reduce their costs enormously and it becomes feasible to develop their own SAAS apps to directly compete with all others. If you combine this with an FTC that looks the other way for a decade or more and allows monopolies to form, this could be a big problem.

Adobe needs to make the market believe, or the LLMs need to make their goals clear

As of now, LLM companies are functioning more as synergistic partners than an enemy of all. If these companies made their stance and goals clear, this would go a long way to help Adobe and other SAAS companies like them.

I think the stock is cheap and a buy. The more I see partnerships with LLMs and continued growth, the more I think this will be an accelerator for great SASS companies rather than a huge problem.

data.title: Two Powerfully Bullish Developments
data.description:
Yesterday, stocks bounced back alongside the cryptocurrency space with Bitcoin regaining the $90,000 level. After six days of gains in the past seven for the S&P 500, I would not be surprised to see some consolidation in the coming days until we move past next week’s rate cut and enter the second half of December. Still, there is likely to be more rotation and positioning in sectors that are poised to rebound in the new year.

Investors may be concerned about abysmal consumer sentiment, bubbles in AI, monetary policy uncertainty, and the constant changes in fiscal policy, but the overriding factor to all of this is that earnings estimates continue to increase. Stock prices track earnings very closely over the long term, and inflection points in a bull market’s trend usually coincide with negative earnings revisions. We don’t have that today. In fact, earnings growth has been accelerating since mid-2023 to 12.9% in the third quarter. According to FactSet, the consensus expectation is for growth of 14.2% in 2026.

I have been noting the improvement in market breadth over the past month, as more stocks in sectors other than technology start to participate in this bull market. That establishes a more solid foundation from which we can see more bull market gains. I love to see technical developments in the market that support my fundamental outlook for the markets and economy. I have two to share today. The Zweig Breadth Thrust is a technical indicator development by famed investor Marty Zweig. It measures the number of advancing stocks on the exchange divided by the total number of advancers and decliners. It then generates a 10-day moving average of this percentage. A “buy” signal is triggered when this moving average rises from below 40% to above 61.5% within a 10-trading day period. Last week, it rose above 59%. While not a buy signal, the strength of the recovery over the past ten days has bullish implications.

In the prior 15 occurrences of rising to 59%, the S&P 500 was higher one year later 14 times with the only loss being 0.1% in 1974. That is a success rate of 93%. The good news doesn’t stop there as the S&P 500 has now risen seven months in a row, which is a rare feat that might suggest we are due for a more significant pullback. Perhaps, but this has happened 16 times since 1950, and the S&P 500 was higher seven months later every single time.

While there are no guarantees we will see a repeat performance, these bullish technical developments embolden my fundamental outlook for an eventual soft landing for the US economy, as the disinflationary trend reasserts itself, and the Federal Reserve moves closer to a neutral rate of interest. Consumer sentiment has likely bottomed, and a recovery will be another important tailwind for investors. I remain on offense and I am looking to position for the broadening in outperformance across various sectors of the market.
